summary.detailed_summary: This paper pioneers EEG-language models (ELMs) by aligning functional brain data with medical textual information for the first time. The authors propose multimodal pretraining on clinical reports and 15,000 EEGs using sub-unit alignment with timeseries cropping and text segmentation, combined with a multiple instance learning extension to address misalignment between irrelevant EEG or text segments. Their multimodal models significantly improve over EEG-only models across four clinical evaluations and enable zero-shot classification as well as retrieval of both neural signals and reports, representing significant progress for clinical applications.

headline_results: 83.10% balanced accuracy for linear probing at 1% labels

key_points: New EEG foundation model: Multimodal pretraining on 15,000 EEGs and clinical reports using sub-unit alignment and multiple instance learning
Method novelty: Combines EEG timeseries cropping with text segmentation and MIL extension to address misalignment between irrelevant segments
Strongest evidence: Zero-shot pathology detection and 9.7% improvement in AUROC over EEG-only models at 1% labeled data
limitations: Limited publicly available paired EEG-report datasets constrain scaling pretraining data
Computational constraints prevented analysis of scaling model sizes
Potential biases from language models may impact multimodal pretraining
Performance ceiling due to inter-rater variability in EEG classification
Need for careful study of how pathology frequency in reports impacts model performance

method.architecture: Residual CNN EEG encoder + Transformer language encoder
method.objective: Contrastive learning with multimodal alignment (ELMe,l) and multiple instance learning extension (ELM-MIL)
method.pretraining: Self-supervised pretraining on 15,000 EEGs with clinical reports using sub-unit alignment

summary.one_liner: EEG-language models (ELMs) pretrained on 15,000 EEGs and clinical reports enable zero-shot pathology detection and outperform EEG-only models for clinical phenotyping.
summary.title: EEG-Language Pretraining for Highly Label-Efficient Clinical Phenotyping
summary.unique_contribution: First application of multimodal pretraining combining natural language and functional brain data in a medical context, achieving highly label-efficient clinical phenotyping through EEG-language models.

papers.title: EEG-Language Pretraining for Highly Label-Efficient Clinical Phenotyping

reasons: Proposes EEG-language models (ELMs) specifically for representation learning
Utilizes pretraining on a large-scale dataset of 15,000 EEGs and clinical reports
Demonstrates foundation-model capabilities including zero-shot classification and cross-modal retrieval

summary.detailed_summary: FoME addresses the challenges of EEG signal heterogeneity and limited labeled data by introducing a foundation model pre-trained on a diverse 1.7TB dataset of scalp and intracranial EEG recordings. The model employs two key innovations: time-frequency fusion embedding and adaptive time-lateral attention scaling (ATLAS) mechanism. These components synergistically capture complex temporal and spectral EEG dynamics, enabling robust multi-channel modeling. Evaluations across four downstream tasks demonstrate superior performance in classification and forecasting applications, achieving state-of-the-art results.

headline_results: State-of-the-art performance across all four downstream tasks

key_points: New EEG foundation model: FoME pre-trained on 1.7TB diverse EEG dataset with 745M parameters for 1,096k steps
Novel time-frequency fusion embedding and ATLAS mechanism capture complex temporal-spectral dynamics across heterogeneous EEG data
Achieves state-of-the-art performance across four downstream tasks including seizure classification, emotion recognition, and signal forecasting
limitations: Pre-training dataset underrepresents certain EEG modalities like emotion-related, sleep, and motor imagery data
Limited representation from cognitive domains like working memory tasks
Underutilization of invasive EEG data which typically offers higher signal-to-noise ratios
Current scale (745M parameters) constrained by available pre-training data
Capabilities for long-term forecasting remain unexplored

method.architecture: Transformer-based with time-frequency fusion embedding and adaptive temporal-lateral attention scaling (ATLAS)
method.finetuning: Fine-tuned for classification, forecasting, and imputation tasks

summary.one_liner: FoME is a large-scale foundation model for EEG that uses adaptive temporal-lateral attention scaling to achieve state-of-the-art performance across multiple downstream tasks.
summary.title: FoME: A Foundation Model for EEG using Adaptive Temporal-Lateral Attention Scaling
summary.unique_contribution: FoME introduces adaptive temporal-lateral attention scaling (ATLAS) that dynamically adapts to changing temporal and spatial patterns across diverse EEG data streams, enabling robust multi-channel modeling without requiring uniform topological rules or custom channel encodings for each dataset.

papers.title: FoME: A Foundation Model for EEG using Adaptive Temporal-Lateral Attention Scaling

reasons: Explicitly proposes 'FoME', a Foundation Model for EEG.
Pre-trained on a large-scale 1.7TB dataset of scalp and intracranial EEG recordings.
Demonstrates broad transferability via evaluation across four different downstream tasks.

summary.title: A Survey of Spatio-Temporal EEG data Analysis: from Models to Applications

papers.title: A Survey of Spatio-Temporal EEG data Analysis: from Models to Applications

reasons: EEG is the primary modality of the survey
Explicitly covers foundation models and LLM-based approaches for EEG analysis
Fits the inclusion criteria for systematic reviews/surveys of EEG-FMs

summary.detailed_summary: The paper proposes the Multi-modal Cross-domain Self-supervised Pre-training (MCSP) model to integrate fMRI and EEG data. It introduces two novel loss functions: CD-SSL for consistency across spatial, temporal, and frequency domains within a modality, and CM-SSL for knowledge distillation between modalities. The model was pre-trained on a large-scale dataset combining five neuroimaging sources and evaluated on ADHD, ASD, and depression classification tasks, demonstrating superior generalizability and performance over existing deep learning baselines.

headline_results: Outperformed baselines including BrainNetCNN, BrainGNN, and Dynamic BNT across multiple diagnostic tasks.

key_points: New EEG foundation model: MCSP integrates EEG and fMRI by synergizing spatial, temporal, and spectral features through self-supervised pre-training.
Employs cross-domain and cross-modal contrastive losses to facilitate knowledge distillation, specifically leveraging fMRI's spatial resolution and EEG's temporal precision.
Demonstrates strong generalizability across cross-modality, cross-dataset, and cross-site scenarios for diagnosing ADHD, ASD, and MDD.
limitations: EEG analysis is restricted to the alpha band, potentially ignoring relevant information in other frequency bands.
The cross-matching strategy for paired data may introduce noise into the pre-training process.
The model architecture requires three separate encoders, increasing parameter count compared to single-domain models.
Downstream evaluation on some datasets involves relatively small sample sizes.

method.architecture: Graph Transformers for spatial encoding and Transformers for temporal/frequency encoding.
method.finetuning: Task-agnostic MLP classifier for downstream diagnosis tasks.
method.objective: CD-SSL (intra- and cross-domain contrastive loss) and CM-SSL (cross-modal distillation and consistency).
method.pretraining: Self-supervised pre-training on multi-modal, multi-domain data.

summary.one_liner: A self-supervised pre-training framework that fuses fMRI and EEG across spatial, temporal, and spectral domains using cross-domain and cross-modal contrastive losses.
summary.title: Multi-modal Cross-domain Self-supervised Pre-training for fMRI and EEG Fusion
summary.unique_contribution: Introduces a dual self-supervised loss mechanism (CD-SSL and CM-SSL) to explicitly model complementary interactions across both different modalities (fMRI/EEG) and different signal domains (spatial/temporal/spectral).

papers.title: Multi-modal Cross-domain Self-supervised Pre-training for fMRI and EEG Fusion

reasons: Proposes a Multi-modal Cross-domain Self-supervised Pre-training Model (MCSP) with EEG as a core modality.
Utilizes a large-scale pre-training dataset to develop a pretrained model.
Explicitly demonstrates generalizability across multiple classification tasks, aligning with foundation model goals.
